title: Musical instrument

infobox.value: is a device created or adapted to make [musical sounds](/wiki/Music "Music"). In principle, any object that produces sound can be considered a musical instrument—it is through purpose that the object becomes a musical instrument. A person who plays a musical instrument is known as an *[instrumentalist](#Instrumentalist)*.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Device for making musical sounds

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Historical_instruments.jpg/250px-Historical_instruments.jpg)](/wiki/File:Historical_instruments.jpg)

Historical İnstruments

A **musical instrument** is a device created or adapted to make [musical sounds](/wiki/Music "Music"). In principle, any object that produces sound can be considered a musical instrument—it is through purpose that the object becomes a musical instrument. A person who plays a musical instrument is known as an *[instrumentalist](#Instrumentalist)*.

The history of musical instruments dates to the beginnings of human culture. Early musical instruments may have been used for rituals, such as a [horn](/wiki/Horn_(music) "Horn (music)") to signal success on the hunt, or a [drum](/wiki/Drum "Drum") in a religious ceremony. Cultures eventually developed composition and performance of [melodies](/wiki/Melody "Melody") for entertainment. Musical instruments evolved in step with changing applications and technologies.

The exact date and specific origin of the first device considered a musical instrument, is widely disputed. The oldest object identified by scholars as a musical instrument, is [a simple flute](/wiki/Divje_Babe_flute "Divje Babe flute"), dated back 50,000–60,000 years. Many scholars date [early flutes](/wiki/Paleolithic_flutes "Paleolithic flutes") to about 40,000 years ago. Many historians believe that determining the specific date of musical instrument invention is impossible, as the majority of early musical instruments were constructed of animal skins, bone, wood, and other non-durable, [bio-degradable](/wiki/Biodegradation "Biodegradation") materials. Additionally, some have proposed that [lithophones](/wiki/Lithophone "Lithophone"), or [stones](/wiki/Rock_(geology) "Rock (geology)") used to make musical sounds—like those found at [Sankarjang](/wiki/Sankarjang "Sankarjang") in India—are examples of prehistoric musical instruments.

Musical instruments developed independently in many populated regions of the world. However, contact among civilizations caused rapid spread and adaptation of most instruments in places far from their origin. By the [post-classical era](/wiki/Post-classical_history "Post-classical history"), instruments from [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia") were in [maritime Southeast Asia](/wiki/Maritime_Southeast_Asia "Maritime Southeast Asia"), and Europeans played instruments originating from [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa"). Development in the Americas occurred at a slower pace, but cultures of [North](/wiki/North_America "North America"), [Central](/wiki/Central_America "Central America"), and [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America") shared musical instruments.

By 1400, musical instrument development slowed in many areas and was dominated by the [Occident](/wiki/Western_world "Western world"). During the [Classical](/wiki/Classical_period_(music) "Classical period (music)") and [Romantic](/wiki/Romantic_music "Romantic music") periods of music, lasting from roughly 1750 to 1900, many new musical instruments were developed. While the evolution of traditional musical instruments slowed beginning in the 20th century, the proliferation of [electricity](/wiki/Electricity "Electricity") led to the invention of new [electric](/wiki/Music_technology_(electric) "Music technology (electric)") and [electronic instruments](/wiki/Electronic_musical_instrument "Electronic musical instrument"), such as [electric guitars](/wiki/Electric_guitar "Electric guitar"), [synthesizers](/wiki/Synthesizer "Synthesizer"), and the [theremin](/wiki/Theremin "Theremin").

Musical instrument classification is a discipline in its own right, and many systems of classification have been used over the years. Instruments can be classified by their effective range, material composition, size, role, etc. However, the most common academic method, [Hornbostel–Sachs](/wiki/Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs "Hornbostel–Sachs"), uses the means by which they produce sound. The academic study of musical instruments is called [organology](/wiki/Organology "Organology").

## Definition and basic operation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Musical_instrument&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Definition and basic operation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/TamTam.jpg/250px-TamTam.jpg)](/wiki/File:TamTam.jpg)

[Bamileke](/wiki/Bamileke "Bamileke") [slit drum](/wiki/Slit_drum "Slit drum") drummers in Cameroon's [West Province](/wiki/West_Province_(Cameroon) "West Province (Cameroon)").

A musical instrument is used to make [musical sounds](/wiki/Music "Music"). Once humans moved from making sounds with their bodies — for example, by clapping—to using objects to create music from sounds, musical instruments were born.[[1]](#cite_note-Montagu1-1)

Primitive instruments were probably designed to emulate [natural sounds](/wiki/Natural_sounds "Natural sounds"), and their purpose was ritual rather than entertainment.[[2]](#cite_note-Rault9-2) The concept of [melody](/wiki/Melody "Melody") and the artistic pursuit of [musical composition](/wiki/Musical_composition "Musical composition") were probably unknown to early players of musical instruments. A person sounding a [bone flute](/wiki/Paleolithic_flutes "Paleolithic flutes") to signal the start of a hunt does so without thought of the modern notion of "making music".[[2]](#cite_note-Rault9-2) Musical instruments are constructed in a broad array of styles and shapes, using many different materials. Early musical instruments were made from "found objects" such as shells and plant parts.[[2]](#cite_note-Rault9-2) As instruments evolved, so did the selection and quality of materials. Virtually every material in nature has been used by at least one culture to make musical instruments.[[2]](#cite_note-Rault9-2)

One plays a musical instrument by interacting with it in some way — for example, by plucking the strings on a [string instrument](/wiki/String_instrument "String instrument"), striking the surface of a [drum](/wiki/Drum "Drum"), or blowing into an animal horn.[[2]](#cite_note-Rault9-2)

## Archaeology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Musical_instrument&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Archaeology")]

Researchers have discovered archaeological evidence of musical instruments in many parts of the world. One disputed [artifact](/wiki/Artifact_(archaeology) "Artifact (archaeology)") (the [Divje Babe flute](/wiki/Divje_Babe_flute "Divje Babe flute")) has been dated to 67,000 years old, but consensus solidifies around artifacts dated back to around 37,000 years old and later. Artifacts made from durable materials or constructed using durable methods, have been found to survive. As such, the specimens found cannot be irrefutably placed as the earliest musical instruments.[[3]](#cite_note-Blades34-3)

Recent studies indicate early [hominins](/wiki/Hominini "Hominini") produced their [percussive instruments](/wiki/Percussion_instrument "Percussion instrument") from such perishable materials as wood and animal hides, which likely the passing of time destroyed the [artifacts](/wiki/Artifact_(archaeology) "Artifact (archaeology)") beyond recovery.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMontagu2007-4)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Fl%C3%BBte_pal%C3%A9olithique_%28mus%C3%A9e_national_de_Slov%C3%A9nie%2C_Ljubljana%29_%289420310527%29.jpg/250px-Fl%C3%BBte_pal%C3%A9olithique_%28mus%C3%A9e_national_de_Slov%C3%A9nie%2C_Ljubljana%29_%289420310527%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fl%C3%BBte_pal%C3%A9olithique_(mus%C3%A9e_national_de_Slov%C3%A9nie,_Ljubljana)_(9420310527).jpg)

Found in [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia"), the [Divje Babe Flute](/wiki/Divje_Babe_Flute "Divje Babe Flute") is sometimes considered the world's [oldest known musical instrument](/wiki/Paleolithic_flutes "Paleolithic flutes")

### Flutes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Musical_instrument&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Flutes")]

The [Divje Babe Flute](/wiki/Divje_Babe_Flute "Divje Babe Flute") is a perforated bone discovered in 1995, in the northwest region of [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia") by archaeologist Ivan Turk. Its origin is disputed, with many arguing that it is most likely the product of carnivores chewing the bone,[[5]](#cite_note-Chase_and_Nowell-5) but Turk and others argue that it is a [Neanderthal](/wiki/Neanderthal "Neanderthal")-made flute. With its age estimated between 43,400 and 67,000 years old, it would be the oldest known musical instrument and the only Neanderthal musical instrument.[[6]](#cite_note-SAS-6)

[Mammoth bone and swan bone flutes](/wiki/Paleolithic_flute "Paleolithic flute") have been found dating back to 30,000 to 37,000 years old in the [Swabian Alps](/wiki/Swabian_Alps "Swabian Alps") of Germany. The flutes were made in the [Upper Paleolithic](/wiki/Upper_Paleolithic "Upper Paleolithic") age, and are more commonly accepted as being the oldest known musical instruments.[[7]](#cite_note-CBC-7)

### Sumerian city of Ur

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Musical_instrument&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Sumerian city of Ur")]

Archaeological evidence of musical instruments was discovered in [excavations](/wiki/Archaeological_excavation "Archaeological excavation") at the [Royal Cemetery](/wiki/Royal_Cemetery_at_Ur "Royal Cemetery at Ur") in the [Sumerian](/wiki/Sumer "Sumer") city of [Ur](/wiki/Ur "Ur"). These instruments, one of the first ensembles of instruments yet discovered, include nine [lyres](/wiki/Lyre "Lyre") (the [Lyres of Ur](/wiki/Lyres_of_Ur "Lyres of Ur")), two [harps](/wiki/Harp "Harp"), a silver double [flute](/wiki/Flute "Flute"), a [sistrum](/wiki/Sistrum "Sistrum"), and [cymbals](/wiki/Cymbal "Cymbal"). A set of reed-sounded silver pipes discovered in Ur was the likely predecessor of modern [bagpipes](/wiki/Bagpipes "Bagpipes").[[8]](#cite_note-collinson-8) The cylindrical pipes feature three side holes that allowed players to produce a [whole-tone scale](/wiki/Whole-tone_scale "Whole-tone scale").[[9]](#cite_note-Campbell82-9) These excavations, carried out by [Leonard Woolley](/wiki/Leonard_Woolley "Leonard Woolley") in the 1920s, uncovered non-degradable fragments of instruments and the voids left by the degraded segments that, together, have been used to reconstruct them.[[10]](#cite_note-de_Schauensee-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) The graves these instruments were buried in have been [carbon dated](/wiki/Carbon_dated "Carbon dated") to between 2600 and 2500 BC, providing evidence that these instruments were used in Sumeria by this time.[[12]](#cite_note-Moorey-12)

### Jiahu site

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Musical_instrument&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Jiahu site")]

Archaeologists in the [Jiahu](/wiki/Jiahu "Jiahu") site of central [Henan province](/wiki/Henan_province "Henan province") of China have found flutes made of bones that date back 7,000 to 9,000 years,[[13]](#cite_note-:0-13) representing some of the "earliest complete, playable, tightly-dated, multinote musical instruments" ever found.[[13]](#cite_note-:0-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) The examination of these instruments reveals they were made with precision to generate specific notes thus showing [Neolithic China's](/wiki/Neolithic_China "Neolithic China") advanced knowledge of music scales and sound engineering.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Musical_instrument&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [History of music](/wiki/History_of_music "History of music")

Scholars agree that there are no completely reliable methods of determining the exact chronology of musical instruments across cultures. Comparing and organizing instruments based on their complexity is misleading, since advancements in musical instruments have sometimes reduced complexity. For example, the construction of early slit drums involved felling and hollowing out large trees; later [slit drums](/wiki/Slit_drum "Slit drum") were made by opening bamboo stalks, a much simpler task.[[16]](#cite_note-Sachs60-16)

German musicologist [Curt Sachs](/wiki/Curt_Sachs "Curt Sachs"), one of the most prominent [musicologists](/wiki/Musicology "Musicology")[[17]](#cite_note-Brown-17) and [musical ethnologists](/wiki/Ethnomusicology "Ethnomusicology")[[18]](#cite_note-Baines37-18) in modern times, argues that it is misleading to arrange the development of musical instruments by workmanship, since cultures advance at different rates and have access to different raw materials.

For example, contemporary [anthropologists](/wiki/Anthropology "Anthropology") comparing musical instruments from two cultures that existed at the same time but differed in organization, culture, and handicraft [cannot determine which instruments are more "primitive"](/wiki/Anthropological_theories_of_value "Anthropological theories of value").[[19]](#cite_note-Sachs61-19)

Ordering instruments by geography is also not reliable, as it cannot always be determined when and how cultures contacted one another and shared knowledge. Sachs proposed that a geographical chronology until approximately 1400 is preferable, however, due to its limited subjectivity.[[20]](#cite_note-Sachs63-20) Beyond 1400, one can follow the overall development of musical instruments over time.[[20]](#cite_note-Sachs63-20)

The science of marking the order of musical instrument development relies on archaeological artifacts, artistic [depictions](/wiki/Depiction "Depiction"), and literary references. Since data in one research path can be inconclusive, all three paths provide a better historical picture.[[3]](#cite_note-Blades34-3)

### Prehistoric

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Musical_instrument&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Prehistoric")]

See also: [Prehistoric music](/wiki/Prehistoric_music "Prehistoric music")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Two_Teponaztli.jpg/250px-Two_Teponaztli.jpg)](/wiki/File:Two_Teponaztli.jpg)

Two [Aztec](/wiki/Aztec "Aztec") slit drums (*[teponaztli](/wiki/Teponaztli "Teponaztli")*). The characteristic "**H**" slits can be seen on the top of the drum in the foreground.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Molo%2C_African_lute.jpg/250px-Molo%2C_African_lute.jpg)](/wiki/File:Molo,_African_lute.jpg)

[Molo](/wiki/Molo_(lute) "Molo (lute)"), a lute of the [Hausa people](/wiki/Hausa_people "Hausa people") of northern Nigeria.

Until the 19th century AD, European-written music histories began with [mythological](/wiki/Myth "Myth") accounts mingled with scripture of how musical instruments were invented. Such accounts included [Jubal](/wiki/Jubal_(Bible) "Jubal (Bible)"), descendant of [Cain](/wiki/Cain "Cain") and "father of all such as handle the harp and the organ" ([Genesis](/wiki/Genesis_creation_narrative "Genesis creation narrative") 4:21) [Pan](/wiki/Pan_(mythology) "Pan (mythology)"), inventor of the [pan pipes](/wiki/Pan_flute "Pan flute"), and [Mercury](/wiki/Mercury_(mythology) "Mercury (mythology)"), who is said to have made a dried [tortoise](/wiki/Tortoise "Tortoise") shell into the first [lyre](/wiki/Lyre "Lyre"). Modern histories have replaced such mythology with anthropological speculation, occasionally informed by archeological evidence. Scholars agree that there was no definitive "invention" of the musical instrument since the term "musical instrument" is subjective and hard to define.[[21]](#cite_note-Sachs297-21)

Among the first devices external to the human body that are considered instruments are [rattles](/wiki/Rattle_(percussion_instrument) "Rattle (percussion instrument)"), stampers, and various [drums](/wiki/Drum "Drum").[[22]](#cite_note-Blades36-22) These instruments evolved due to the human motor impulse to add sound to emotional movements such as dancing.[[23]](#cite_note-Sachs26-23) Eventually, some cultures assigned ritual functions to their musical instruments, using them for hunting and various ceremonies.[[24]](#cite_note-Rault34-24) Those cultures developed more complex [percussion instruments](/wiki/Percussion_instrument "Percussion instrument") and other instruments such as ribbon reeds, flutes, and trumpets. Some of these labels carry far different connotations from those used in modern day; early flutes and trumpets are so-labeled for their basic operation and function rather than resemblance to modern instruments.[[25]](#cite_note-Sachs34-25) Among early cultures for whom drums developed ritual, even sacred importance are the [Chukchi people](/wiki/Chukchi_people "Chukchi people") of the [Russian Far East](/wiki/Russian_Far_East "Russian Far East"), the indigenous people of [Melanesia](/wiki/Melanesia "Melanesia"), and many cultures of [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa"). In fact, drums were pervasive throughout every African culture.[[26]](#cite_note-Blades51-26) One East African tribe, the [Wahinda](/wiki/Wahinda "Wahinda"), believed it was so holy that seeing a drum would be fatal to any person other than the [sultan](/wiki/Sultan "Sultan").[[27]](#cite_note-Sachs35-27)

Humans eventually developed the concept of using musical instruments to produce [melody](/wiki/Melody "Melody"), which was previously common only in singing. Similar to the process of [reduplication](/wiki/Reduplication "Reduplication") in language, instrument players first developed repetition and then arrangement. An early form of melody was produced by pounding two stamping tubes of slightly different sizes—one tube would produce a "clear" sound and the other would answer with a "darker" sound. Such instrument pairs also included [bullroarers](/wiki/Bullroarer_(music) "Bullroarer (music)"), slit drums, [shell trumpets](/wiki/Conch_(instrument) "Conch (instrument)"), and skin drums. Cultures who used these instrument pairs associated them with gender; the "father" was the bigger or more energetic instrument, while the "mother" was the smaller or duller instrument. Musical instruments existed in this form for thousands of years before patterns of three or more tones would evolve in the form of the earliest [xylophone](/wiki/Xylophone "Xylophone").[[28]](#cite_note-Sachs52-28) Xylophones originated in the mainland and [archipelago](/wiki/Archipelago "Archipelago") of [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia"), eventually spreading to Africa, the Americas, and Europe.[[29]](#cite_note-Marcuse24-29) Along with xylophones, which ranged from simple sets of three "leg bars" to carefully tuned sets of parallel bars, various cultures developed instruments such as the [ground harp](/wiki/Ground_harp "Ground harp"), [ground zither](/wiki/Ground_zither "Ground zither"), [musical bow](/wiki/Musical_bow "Musical bow"), and [jaw harp](/wiki/Jew%27s_harp "Jew's harp").[[30]](#cite_note-Sachs53-30) Recent research into usage wear and acoustics of stone artefacts has revealed a possible new class of prehistoric musical instrument, known as [lithophones](/wiki/Lithophone "Lithophone").[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)

### Antiquity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Musical_instrument&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Antiquity")]

See also: [Ancient music](/wiki/Ancient_music "Ancient music")

Images of musical instruments begin to appear in Mesopotamian artifacts in 2800 BC or earlier. Beginning around 2000 BC, [Sumerian](/wiki/Sumer "Sumer") and [Babylonian](/wiki/Babylon "Babylon") cultures began delineating two distinct classes of musical instruments due to [division of labor](/wiki/Division_of_labor "Division of labor") and the evolving [class system](/wiki/Social_class "Social class"). Popular instruments, simple and playable by anyone, evolved differently from professional instruments whose development focused on effectiveness and skill.[[33]](#cite_note-Sachs67-33) Despite this development, very few musical instruments have been recovered in [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia"). Scholars must rely on artifacts and [cuneiform](/wiki/Cuneiform "Cuneiform") texts written in [Sumerian](/wiki/Sumerian_language "Sumerian language") or [Akkadian](/wiki/Akkadian_language "Akkadian language") to reconstruct the early history of musical instruments in Mesopotamia. Even the process of assigning names to these instruments is challenging since there is no clear distinction among various instruments and the words used to describe them.[[34]](#cite_note-Sachs68-34)

Although Sumerian and Babylonian artists mainly depicted ceremonial instruments, historians have distinguished six [idiophones](/wiki/Idiophone "Idiophone") used in early Mesopotamia: concussion clubs, [clappers](/wiki/Clapper_(musical_instrument) "Clapper (musical instrument)"), [sistra](/wiki/Sistrum "Sistrum"), bells, cymbals, and [rattles](/wiki/Rattle_(percussion_instrument) "Rattle (percussion instrument)").[[35]](#cite_note-Sachs69-35) Sistra are depicted prominently in a great relief of [Amenhotep III](/wiki/Amenhotep_III "Amenhotep III"),[[36]](#cite_note-Remnant168-36) and are of particular interest because similar designs have been found in far-reaching places such as [Tbilisi](/wiki/Tbilisi "Tbilisi"), [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(country) "Georgia (country)") and among the [Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") [Yaqui](/wiki/Yaqui "Yaqui") tribe.[[37]](#cite_note-Sachs70-37) The people of Mesopotamia preferred [stringed instruments](/wiki/String_instrument "String instrument"), as evidenced by their proliferation in Mesopotamian figurines, plaques, and seals. Innumerable varieties of harps are depicted, as well as lyres and lutes, the forerunner of modern stringed instruments such as the [violin](/wiki/Violin "Violin").[[38]](#cite_note-Sachs82-38)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Egyptian_lute_players_001.jpg/250px-Egyptian_lute_players_001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Egyptian_lute_players_001.jpg)

[Ancient Egyptian](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt") tomb painting depicting lute players, [18th Dynasty](/wiki/Eighteenth_dynasty_of_Egypt "Eighteenth dynasty of Egypt") (c. 1350 BC)

Musical instruments used by the Egyptian culture before 2700 BC bore striking similarity to those of Mesopotamia, leading historians to conclude that the civilizations must have been in contact with one another. Sachs notes that Egypt did not possess any instruments that the Sumerian culture did not also possess.[[39]](#cite_note-Sachs86-39) However, by 2700 BC the cultural contacts seem to have dissipated; the lyre, a prominent ceremonial instrument in Sumer, did not appear in Egypt for another 800 years.[[39]](#cite_note-Sachs86-39) Clappers and concussion sticks appear on Egyptian vases as early as 3000 BC. The civilization also made use of sistra, [vertical flutes](/wiki/Vertical_flute "Vertical flute"), [double clarinets](/wiki/Double_clarinet "Double clarinet"), arched and angular harps, and various drums.[[40]](#cite_note-Rault71-40)

Little history is available in the period between 2700 BC and 1500 BC, as Egypt (and indeed, Babylon) entered a long violent period of war and destruction. This period saw the [Kassites](/wiki/Kassites "Kassites") destroy the [Babylonian empire](/wiki/Babylonia "Babylonia") in Mesopotamia and the [Hyksos](/wiki/Hyksos "Hyksos") destroy the [Middle Kingdom of Egypt](/wiki/Middle_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Middle Kingdom of Egypt"). When the [Pharaohs of Egypt](/wiki/Pharaoh "Pharaoh") conquered Southwest Asia in around 1500 BC, the cultural ties to Mesopotamia were renewed and Egypt's musical instruments also reflected heavy influence from Asiatic cultures.[[39]](#cite_note-Sachs86-39) Under their new cultural influences, the people of the [New Kingdom](/wiki/New_Kingdom_of_Egypt "New Kingdom of Egypt") began using [oboes](/wiki/Oboe "Oboe"), trumpets, [lyres](/wiki/Lyre "Lyre"), [lutes](/wiki/Lute "Lute"), [castanets](/wiki/Castanets "Castanets"), and [cymbals](/wiki/Cymbal "Cymbal").[[41]](#cite_note-Sachs98-41)

Unlike Mesopotamia and Egypt, professional musicians did not exist in [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") between 2000 and 1000 BC. While the history of musical instruments in Mesopotamia and Egypt relies on artistic representations, the culture in Israel produced few such representations. Scholars must therefore rely on information gleaned from the [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible") and the [Talmud](/wiki/Talmud "Talmud").[[42]](#cite_note-Sachs105-42) The [Hebrew texts](/wiki/Hebrew_literature "Hebrew literature") mention two prominent instruments associated with [Jubal](/wiki/Jubal_(Bible) "Jubal (Bible)"): the *ugab* (pipes) and *[kinnor](/wiki/Kinnor "Kinnor")* (lyre).[[43]](#cite_note-Sachs106-43) Other instruments of the period included the *tof* ([frame drum](/wiki/Frame_drum "Frame drum")), *pa'amon* (small bells or jingles), [shofar](/wiki/Shofar "Shofar"), and the trumpet-like *hasosra*.[[44]](#cite_note-Sachs108-44)

The introduction of a monarchy in Israel during the 11th century BC produced the first professional musicians and with them a drastic increase in the number and variety of musical instruments.[[45]](#cite_note-Sachs114-45) However, identifying and classifying the instruments remains a challenge due to the lack of artistic interpretations. For example, stringed instruments of uncertain design called nevals and asors existed, but neither archaeology nor etymology can clearly define them.[[46]](#cite_note-Sachs116-46) In her book *A Survey of Musical Instruments*, American musicologist Sibyl Marcuse proposes that the nevel must be similar to vertical harp due to its relation to *nabla*, the [Phoenician](/wiki/Phoenicia "Phoenicia") term for "harp".[[47]](#cite_note-Marcuse385-47)

In [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece"), [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), and [Etruria](/wiki/Etruria "Etruria"), the use and development of musical instruments stood in stark contrast to those cultures' achievements in architecture and sculpture. The instruments of the time were simple and virtually all of them were imported from other cultures.[[48]](#cite_note-Sachs128-48) Lyres were the principal instrument, as musicians used them to honor the gods.[[49]](#cite_note-Sachs129-49) Greeks played a variety of [wind instruments](/wiki/Wind_instrument "Wind instrument") they classified as *[aulos](/wiki/Aulos "Aulos")* (reeds) or *[syrinx](/wiki/Syrinx_(instrument) "Syrinx (instrument)")* (flutes); Greek writing from that time reflects a serious study of reed production and playing technique.[[9]](#cite_note-Campbell82-9) Romans played reed instruments named *[tibia](/wiki/Tibia_(instrument) "Tibia (instrument)")*, featuring side-holes that could be opened or closed, allowing for greater flexibility in playing modes.[[50]](#cite_note-Campbell83-50) Other instruments in common use in the region included vertical harps derived from those of the [Orient](/wiki/Orient "Orient"), lutes of Egyptian design, various pipes and organs, and clappers, which were played primarily by women.[[51]](#cite_note-Sachs149-51)

Evidence of musical instruments in use by early civilizations of [India](/wiki/India "India") is almost completely lacking, making it impossible to reliably attribute instruments to the [Munda](/wiki/Munda_languages "Munda languages") and [Dravidian](/wiki/Dravidian_languages "Dravidian languages") language-speaking cultures that first settled the area. Rather, the history of musical instruments in the area begins with the [Indus Valley civilization](/wiki/Indus_Valley_civilization "Indus Valley civilization") that emerged around 3000 BC. Various rattles and whistles found among excavated artifacts are the only physical evidence of musical instruments.[[52]](#cite_note-Sachs151-52) A clay statuette indicates the use of drums, and examination of the [Indus script](/wiki/Indus_script "Indus script") has also revealed representations of vertical arched harps identical in design to those depicted in Sumerian artifacts. This discovery is among many indications that the Indus Valley and Sumerian cultures maintained cultural contact. Subsequent developments in musical instruments in India occurred with the [Rigveda](/wiki/Rigveda "Rigveda"), or hymns. These songs used various drums, shell trumpets, harps, and flutes.[[53]](#cite_note-Sachs152-53) Other prominent instruments in use during the early centuries AD were the [snake charmer's](/wiki/Snake_charming "Snake charming") [double clarinet](/wiki/Double_clarinet "Double clarinet"), [bagpipes](/wiki/Bagpipes "Bagpipes"), barrel drums, cross flutes, and short lutes. In all, India had no unique musical instruments until the [post-classical era](/wiki/Post-classical_history "Post-classical history").[[54]](#cite_note-Sachs161-54)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/%E6%B9%96%E5%8C%97%E5%8D%9A%E7%89%A9%E9%A4%A8%E6%9B%BE%E4%BE%AF%E4%B9%99%E7%B7%A8%E9%90%98.jpg/250px-%E6%B9%96%E5%8C%97%E5%8D%9A%E7%89%A9%E9%A4%A8%E6%9B%BE%E4%BE%AF%E4%B9%99%E7%B7%A8%E9%90%98.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E6%B9%96%E5%8C%97%E5%8D%9A%E7%89%A9%E9%A4%A8%E6%9B%BE%E4%BE%AF%E4%B9%99%E7%B7%A8%E9%90%98.jpg)

The monumental [Bianzhong of Marquis Yi of Zeng](/wiki/Bianzhong_of_Marquis_Yi_of_Zeng "Bianzhong of Marquis Yi of Zeng"), c. 5th century BCE, from [Hubei](/wiki/Hubei "Hubei")

Musical instruments such as [zithers](/wiki/Zither "Zither") appeared in Chinese writings around 12th century BC and earlier.[[55]](#cite_note-Sachs185-55) Early [Chinese philosophers](/wiki/Chinese_philosophy "Chinese philosophy") such as [Confucius](/wiki/Confucius "Confucius") (551–479 BC), [Mencius](/wiki/Mencius "Mencius") (372–289 BC), and [Laozi](/wiki/Laozi "Laozi") shaped the development of musical instruments in China, adopting an attitude toward music similar to that of the Greeks. The Chinese believed that music was an essential part of character and community, and developed a unique system of classifying their musical instruments according to their material makeup.[[56]](#cite_note-Sachs162-56) In Vietnam, an archaeological discovery of a 2,000-year old stringed instrument gives important insights on early chordophones in Southeast Asia.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

[Idiophones](/wiki/Idiophone "Idiophone") were extremely important in Chinese music, hence the majority of early instruments were idiophones. Poetry of the [Shang dynasty](/wiki/Shang_dynasty "Shang dynasty") mentions bells, chimes, drums, and globular flutes carved from bone, the latter of which has been excavated and preserved by archaeologists.[[58]](#cite_note-Sachs166-58) The [Zhou dynasty](/wiki/Zhou_dynasty "Zhou dynasty") saw percussion instruments such as [clappers](/wiki/Clapper_(musical_instrument) "Clapper (musical instrument)"), troughs, [wooden fish](/wiki/Wooden_fish "Wooden fish"), and *[yǔ](/wiki/Yu_(percussion_instrument) "Yu (percussion instrument)")* (wooden tiger). Wind instruments such as flute, [pan-pipes](/wiki/Pan-pipe "Pan-pipe"), [pitch-pipes](/wiki/Pitch-pipe "Pitch-pipe"), and mouth organs also appeared in this time period.[[59]](#cite_note-Sachs178-59) The *[xiao](/wiki/Xiao_(flute) "Xiao (flute)")* (an [end-blown flute](/wiki/End-blown_flute "End-blown flute")) and various other instruments that spread through many cultures, came into use in China during and after the [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty").[[60]](#cite_note-Sachs189-60)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/CarnyxDeTintignac2.jpg/250px-CarnyxDeTintignac2.jpg)](/wiki/File:CarnyxDeTintignac2.jpg)

[Carnyx](/wiki/Carnyx "Carnyx") discovered in [Tintignac](/wiki/Tintignac "Tintignac")

Although civilizations in [Central America](/wiki/Central_America "Central America") attained a relatively high level of sophistication by the eleventh century AD, they lagged behind other civilizations in the development of musical instruments. For example, they had no stringed instruments; all of their instruments were idiophones, drums, and wind instruments such as flutes and trumpets. Of these, only the flute was capable of producing a melody.[[61]](#cite_note-Sachs192-61) In contrast, [pre-Columbian](/wiki/Pre-Columbian "Pre-Columbian") [South American](/wiki/South_America "South America") civilizations in areas such as modern-day [Peru](/wiki/Peru "Peru"), [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia "Colombia"), [Ecuador](/wiki/Ecuador "Ecuador"), [Bolivia](/wiki/Bolivia "Bolivia"), and [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile") were less advanced culturally but more advanced musically. South American cultures of the time used pan-pipes as well as varieties of flutes, idiophones, drums, and shell or wood trumpets.[[62]](#cite_note-Sachs196-62)

An instrument that can be attested to the [Iron Age](/wiki/Iron_Age "Iron Age") [Celts](/wiki/Celts "Celts") is the [carnyx](/wiki/Carnyx "Carnyx"), which is dated to c.300 BC. The end of the bell, which was crafted from bronze, was into the shape of a screaming animal head which was held high above their heads. When blown into, the carnyx would emit a deep, harsh sound; the head also had a tongue which clicked when vibrated. It is believed the intention of the instrument was to use it on the battleground to intimidate their opponents.[[63]](#cite_note-63)[[64]](#cite_note-64)

### Post-classical era/Middle Ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Musical_instrument&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Post-classical era/Middle Ages")]

During the period of time loosely referred to as the [post-classical era](/wiki/Post-classical_history "Post-classical history") and Europe in particular as the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), China developed a tradition of integrating musical influence from other regions. The first record of this type of influence is in 384 AD, when China established an orchestra in its imperial court after a conquest in [Turkestan](/wiki/Turkestan "Turkestan"). Influences from [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East"), [Persia](/wiki/Iran "Iran"), [India](/wiki/India "India"), [Mongolia](/wiki/Mongolia "Mongolia"), and other countries followed. In fact, Chinese tradition attributes many musical instruments from this period to those regions and countries.[[65]](#cite_note-Sachs207-65) Cymbals gained popularity, along with more advanced trumpets, clarinets, pianos, oboes, flutes, drums, and lutes.[[66]](#cite_note-Sachs218-66) Some of the first [bowed](/wiki/Bow_(music) "Bow (music)") zithers appeared in China in the 9th or 10th century, influenced by Mongolian culture.[[67]](#cite_note-Sachs216-67)

India experienced similar development to China in the post-classical era; however, stringed instruments developed differently as they accommodated different styles of music. While stringed instruments of China were designed to produce precise tones capable of matching the tones of chimes, stringed instruments of India were considerably more flexible. This flexibility suited the slides and [tremolos](/wiki/Tremolo "Tremolo") of Hindu music. Rhythm was of paramount importance in Indian music of the time, as evidenced by the frequent depiction of drums in reliefs dating to the post-classical era. The emphasis on rhythm is an aspect native to Indian music.[[68]](#cite_note-Sachs221-68) Historians divide the development of musical instruments in medieval India between pre-Islamic and Islamic periods due to the different influence each period provided.[[69]](#cite_note-Sachs222-69)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Alboka.png/250px-Alboka.png)](/wiki/File:Alboka.png)

The Alboka has a double-reed that vibrates when blown on the small tube. The tubes regulates the melody and the big horn amplifies the sound.

In pre-Islamic times, idiophones such as [handbells](/wiki/Handbell "Handbell"), cymbals, and peculiar instruments resembling gongs came into wide use in Hindu music. The gong-like instrument was a bronze disk that was struck with a hammer instead of a mallet. Tubular drums, stick zithers ([veena](/wiki/Veena "Veena")), short fiddles, double and triple flutes, coiled trumpets, and curved India horns emerged in this time period.[[70]](#cite_note-Sachs223-70) Islamic influences brought new types of drum, perfectly circular or octagonal as opposed to the irregular pre-Islamic drums.[[71]](#cite_note-Sachs229-71) Persian influence brought oboes and [sitars](/wiki/Sitar "Sitar"), although Persian sitars had three strings and Indian version had from four to seven.[[72]](#cite_note-Sachs231-72) The Islamic culture also introduced double-[clarinet](/wiki/Clarinet "Clarinet") instruments as the [Alboka](/wiki/Alboka "Alboka") (from Arab, al-buq or "horn") nowadays only alive in [Basque Country](/wiki/Basque_Country_(autonomous_community) "Basque Country (autonomous community)"). It must be played using the technique of the circular breathing.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Traditional_indonesian_instruments.jpg/250px-Traditional_indonesian_instruments.jpg)](/wiki/File:Traditional_indonesian_instruments.jpg)

An Indonesian metallophone

Southeast Asian musical innovations include those during a period of Indian influence that ended around 920 AD.[[73]](#cite_note-Sachs236-73) [Balinese](/wiki/Balinese_people "Balinese people") and [Javanese](/wiki/Javanese_people "Javanese people") music made use of [xylophones](/wiki/Xylophone "Xylophone") and [metallophones](/wiki/Metallophone "Metallophone"), bronze versions of the former.[[74]](#cite_note-Sachs238-74) The most prominent and important musical instrument of Southeast Asia was the gong. While the gong likely originated in the geographical area between [Tibet](/wiki/Tibet "Tibet") and [Burma](/wiki/Burma "Burma"), it was part of every category of human activity in [maritime Southeast Asia](/wiki/Maritime_Southeast_Asia "Maritime Southeast Asia") including [Java](/wiki/Java "Java").[[75]](#cite_note-Sachs240-75)

The areas of Mesopotamia and the [Arabian Peninsula](/wiki/Arabian_Peninsula "Arabian Peninsula") experiences rapid growth and sharing of musical instruments once they were united by [Islamic culture](/wiki/Islamic_culture "Islamic culture") in the seventh century.[[76]](#cite_note-Sachs246-76) Frame drums and cylindrical drums of various depths were immensely important in all genres of music.[[77]](#cite_note-Sachs249-77) Conical oboes were involved in the music that accompanied wedding and circumcision ceremonies. Persian miniatures provide information on the development of [kettle drums](/wiki/Timpani "Timpani") in Mesopotamia that spread as far as Java.[[78]](#cite_note-Sachs250-78) Various lutes, zithers, [dulcimers](/wiki/Hammered_dulcimer "Hammered dulcimer"), and harps spread as far as [Madagascar](/wiki/Madagascar "Madagascar") to the south and modern-day [Sulawesi](/wiki/Sulawesi "Sulawesi") to the east.[[79]](#cite_note-Sachs251-79)

Despite the influences of Greece and Rome, most musical instruments in Europe during the Middles Ages came from Asia. The lyre is the only musical instrument that may have been invented in Europe until this period.[[80]](#cite_note-Sachs260-80) Stringed instruments were prominent in Middle Age Europe. The central and northern regions used mainly lutes, stringed instruments with [necks](/wiki/Neck_(music) "Neck (music)"), while the southern region used lyres, which featured a two-armed body and a crossbar.[[80]](#cite_note-Sachs260-80) Various harps served Central and Northern Europe as far north as Ireland, where the harp eventually became a national symbol.[[81]](#cite_note-Sachs263-81) Lyres propagated through the same areas, as far east as [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia").[[82]](#cite_note-Sachs265-82)

European music between 800 and 1100 became more sophisticated, more frequently requiring instruments capable of [polyphony](/wiki/Polyphony "Polyphony"). The 9th-century [Persian](/wiki/Persian_people "Persian people") geographer [Ibn Khordadbeh](/wiki/Ibn_Khordadbeh "Ibn Khordadbeh") mentioned in his lexicographical discussion of music instruments that, in the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire"), typical instruments included the *urghun* ([organ](/wiki/Organ_(music) "Organ (music)")), *shilyani* (probably a type of [harp](/wiki/Harp "Harp") or [lyre](/wiki/Lyre "Lyre")), *salandj* (probably a [bagpipe](/wiki/Bagpipe "Bagpipe")) and the [lyra](/wiki/Byzantine_lyra "Byzantine lyra").[[83]](#cite_note-Kartomi124-83) The Byzantine lyra, a [bowed](/wiki/Bow_(music) "Bow (music)") string instrument, is an ancestor of most European bowed instruments, including the [violin](/wiki/Violin "Violin").[[84]](#cite_note-Grillet29-84)

The [monochord](/wiki/Monochord "Monochord") served as a precise measure of the notes of a musical scale, allowing more accurate musical arrangements.[[85]](#cite_note-Sachs269-85) Mechanical [hurdy-gurdies](/wiki/Hurdy-gurdy "Hurdy-gurdy") allowed single musicians to play more complicated arrangements than a fiddle would; both were prominent folk instruments in the Middle Ages.[[86]](#cite_note-Sachs271-86)[[87]](#cite_note-Sachs274-87) Southern Europeans played short and long lutes whose pegs extended to the sides, unlike the rear-facing pegs of Central and Northern European instruments.[[88]](#cite_note-Sachs273-88) Idiophones such as bells and clappers served various practical purposes, such as warning of the approach of a [leper](/wiki/Leprosy "Leprosy").[[89]](#cite_note-Sachs278-89)

The ninth century revealed the first [bagpipes](/wiki/Bagpipes "Bagpipes"), which spread throughout Europe and had many uses from folk instruments to military instruments.[[90]](#cite_note-Sachs281-90) The construction of pneumatic [organs](/wiki/Organ_(music) "Organ (music)") evolved in Europe starting in fifth-century [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"), spreading to [England](/wiki/England "England") in about 700.[[91]](#cite_note-Sachs284-91) The resulting instruments varied in size and use from portable organs worn around the neck to large pipe organs.[[92]](#cite_note-Sachs286-92) Literary accounts of organs being played in English [Benedictine](/wiki/Benedictine "Benedictine") abbeys toward the end of the tenth century are the first references to organs being connected to churches.[[93]](#cite_note-Bicknell13-93) Reed players of the Middle Ages were limited to [oboes](/wiki/Oboe "Oboe"); no evidence of [clarinets](/wiki/Clarinet "Clarinet") exists during this period.[[94]](#cite_note-Sachs288-94)

### Modern

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Musical_instrument&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Modern")]

#### Western Classical

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Musical_instrument&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Western Classical")]

##### Renaissance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Musical_instrument&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Renaissance")]

Musical instrument development was dominated by the [Occident](/wiki/Western_world "Western world") from 1400 on, indeed, the most profound changes occurred during the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") period.[[21]](#cite_note-Sachs297-21) Instruments took on other purposes than accompanying singing or dance, and performers used them as solo instruments. Keyboards and lutes developed as [polyphonic](/wiki/Polyphonic "Polyphonic") instruments, and composers arranged increasingly complex pieces using more advanced [tablature](/wiki/Tablature "Tablature"). Composers also began designing pieces of music for specific instruments.[[21]](#cite_note-Sachs297-21) In the latter half of the sixteenth century, [orchestration](/wiki/Orchestration "Orchestration") came into common practice as a method of writing music for a variety of instruments. Composers now specified orchestration where individual performers once applied their own discretion.[[95]](#cite_note-Sachs298-95) The polyphonic style dominated popular music, and the instrument makers responded accordingly.[[96]](#cite_note-Sachs351-96)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/The_Duet_c1635_by_Saftleven.jpg/250px-The_Duet_c1635_by_Saftleven.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Duet_c1635_by_Saftleven.jpg)

*The Duet*, by Dutch painter [Cornelis Saftleven](/wiki/Cornelis_Saftleven "Cornelis Saftleven"), c. 1635. It shows a [violinist](/wiki/Violin "Violin") and a [cittern](/wiki/Cittern "Cittern") player.

Beginning in about 1400, the rate of development of musical instruments increased in earnest as compositions demanded more dynamic sounds. People also began writing books about creating, playing, and cataloging musical instruments; the first such book was [Sebastian Virdung](/wiki/Sebastian_Virdung "Sebastian Virdung")'s 1511 treatise *Musica getuscht und ausgezogen* ('Music Germanized and Abstracted').[[95]](#cite_note-Sachs298-95) Virdung's work is noted as being particularly thorough for including descriptions of "irregular" instruments such as hunters' horns and cow bells, though Virdung is critical of the same. Other books followed, including [Arnolt Schlick](/wiki/Arnolt_Schlick "Arnolt Schlick")'s *Spiegel der Orgelmacher und Organisten* ('Mirror of Organ Makers and Organ Players') the following year, a treatise on organ building and organ playing.[[97]](#cite_note-Sachs299-97) Of the instructional books and references published in the Renaissance era, one is noted for its detailed description and depiction of all wind and stringed instruments, including their relative sizes. This book, the *Syntagma musicum* by [Michael Praetorius](/wiki/Michael_Praetorius "Michael Praetorius"), is now considered an authoritative reference of sixteenth-century musical instruments.[[98]](#cite_note-Sachs301-98)

In the sixteenth century, musical instrument builders gave most instruments – such as the violin – the "classical shapes" they retain today. An emphasis on aesthetic beauty also developed; listeners were as pleased with the physical appearance of an instrument as they were with its sound. Therefore, builders paid special attention to materials and workmanship, and instruments became collectibles in homes and museums.[[99]](#cite_note-Sachs302-99) It was during this period that makers began constructing instruments of the same type in various sizes to meet the demand of *consorts*, or ensembles playing works written for these groups of instruments.[[100]](#cite_note-Sachs303-100)

Instrument builders developed other features that endure today. For example, while organs with multiple keyboards and pedals already existed, the first organs with [solo stops](/wiki/Organ_stop "Organ stop") emerged in the early fifteenth century. These stops were meant to produce a mixture of timbres, a development needed for the complexity of music of the time.[[101]](#cite_note-Sachs307-101) Trumpets evolved into their modern form to improve portability, and players used [mutes](/wiki/Mute_(music) "Mute (music)") to properly blend into [chamber music](/wiki/Chamber_music "Chamber music").[[102]](#cite_note-Sachs328-102)

##### Baroque

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Musical_instrument&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Baroque")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Stainer.jpg/250px-Stainer.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stainer.jpg)

[Baroque](/wiki/Baroque "Baroque") mounted [Jacob Stainer](/wiki/Jacob_Stainer "Jacob Stainer") violin from 1658

Beginning in the seventeenth century, composers began writing works to a higher emotional degree. They felt that [polyphony](/wiki/Polyphony "Polyphony") better suited the emotional style they were aiming for and began writing musical parts for instruments that would complement the singing human voice.[[96]](#cite_note-Sachs351-96) As a result, many instruments that were incapable of larger ranges and dynamics, and therefore were seen as unemotional, fell out of favor. One such instrument was the shawm.[[103]](#cite_note-Sachs352-103) Bowed instruments such as the [violin](/wiki/Violin "Violin"), [viola](/wiki/Viola "Viola"), [baryton](/wiki/Baryton "Baryton"), and various lutes dominated popular music.[[104]](#cite_note-Sachs353-104) Beginning in around 1750, however, the lute disappeared from musical compositions in favor of the rising popularity of the guitar.[[105]](#cite_note-Sachs374-105) As the prevalence of [string orchestras](/wiki/String_orchestra "String orchestra") rose, wind instruments such as the flute, oboe, and bassoon were readmitted to counteract the monotony of hearing only strings.[[106]](#cite_note-Sachs380-106)

In the mid-seventeenth century, what was known as a hunter's horn underwent a transformation into an "art instrument" consisting of a lengthened tube, a narrower bore, a wider bell, and a much wider range. The details of this transformation are unclear, but the modern [horn](/wiki/French_horn "French horn") or, more colloquially, French horn, had emerged by 1725.[[107]](#cite_note-Sachs384-107) The [slide trumpet](/wiki/Slide_trumpet "Slide trumpet") appeared, a variation that includes a long-throated mouthpiece that slid in and out, allowing the player infinite adjustments in [pitch](/wiki/Pitch_(music) "Pitch (music)"). This variation on the trumpet was unpopular due to the difficulty involved in playing it.[[108]](#cite_note-Sachs385-108) Organs underwent tonal changes in the Baroque period, as manufacturers such as Abraham Jordan of London made the stops more expressive and added devices such as expressive pedals. Sachs viewed this trend as a "degeneration" of the general organ sound.[[109]](#cite_note-Sachs386-109)

##### Classical and Romantic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Musical_instrument&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Classical and Romantic")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Mozart_family_crop.jpg/250px-Mozart_family_crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mozart_family_crop.jpg)

[Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart](/wiki/Wolfgang_Amadeus_Mozart "Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart") plays the keyboard while his father, [Leopold Mozart](/wiki/Leopold_Mozart "Leopold Mozart"), plays violin.

During the [Classical](/wiki/Classical_period_(music) "Classical period (music)") and [Romantic](/wiki/Romantic_music "Romantic music") periods of music, lasting from roughly 1750 to 1900, many musical instruments capable of producing new timbres and higher volume were developed and introduced into popular music. The design changes that broadened the quality of timbres allowed instruments to produce a wider variety of expression. Large orchestras rose in popularity and, in parallel, the composers determined to produce entire orchestral scores that made use of the expressive abilities of modern instruments. Since instruments were involved in collaborations of a much larger scale, their designs had to evolve to accommodate the demands of the orchestra.[[110]](#cite_note-Sachs388-110)

Some instruments also had to become louder to fill larger halls and be heard over sizable orchestras. Flutes and bowed instruments underwent many modifications and design changes—most of them unsuccessful—in efforts to increase volume. Other instruments were changed just so they could play their parts in the scores. Trumpets traditionally had a "defective" range—they were incapable of producing certain notes with precision.[[111]](#cite_note-Sachs389-111) New instruments such as the [clarinet](/wiki/Clarinet "Clarinet"), [saxophone](/wiki/Saxophone "Saxophone"), and [tuba](/wiki/Tuba "Tuba") became fixtures in orchestras. Instruments such as the clarinet also grew into entire "families" of instruments capable of different ranges: small clarinets, normal clarinets, bass clarinets, and so on.[[110]](#cite_note-Sachs388-110)

[![A "young boy playing the violin." Beside him is a table with likely a banjo on it.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Young_boy_playing_the_violin_%28I0002501%29.tiff/lossy-page1-250px-Young_boy_playing_the_violin_%28I0002501%29.tiff.jpg)](/wiki/File:Young_boy_playing_the_violin_(I0002501).tiff)

A "young boy playing the violin" from Glengarry County, Ontario taken [between 1895 and 1910] from the Bartle Brothers fonds at the Archives of Ontario.

Accompanying the changes to timbre and volume was a shift in the typical pitch used to tune instruments. Instruments meant to play together, as in an orchestra, must be tuned to the same standard lest they produce audibly different sounds while playing the same notes. Beginning in 1762, the average [concert pitch](/wiki/Concert_pitch "Concert pitch") began rising from a low of 377 vibrations to a high of 457 in 1880 Vienna.[[112]](#cite_note-Sachs390-112) Different regions, countries, and even instrument manufacturers preferred different standards, making orchestral collaboration a challenge. Despite even the efforts of two organized international summits attended by noted composers like [Hector Berlioz](/wiki/Hector_Berlioz "Hector Berlioz"), no standard could be agreed upon.[[113]](#cite_note-Sachs391-113)

#### Twentieth century to present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Musical_instrument&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Twentieth century to present")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/FGF_museum_01._Leo_and_early_models.jpg/250px-FGF_museum_01._Leo_and_early_models.jpg)](/wiki/File:FGF_museum_01._Leo_and_early_models.jpg)

Early [Fender](/wiki/Fender_Musical_Instruments_Corporation "Fender Musical Instruments Corporation") brand [electric guitars](/wiki/Electric_guitar "Electric guitar")

The evolution of traditional musical instruments slowed beginning in the 20th century.[[114]](#cite_note-Remnant183-114) Instruments such as the violin, flute, french horn, and harp are largely the same as those manufactured throughout the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries. Gradual iterations do emerge; for example, the "New Violin Family" began in 1964 to provide differently sized violins to expand the range of available sounds.[[115]](#cite_note-Remnant70-115) The slowdown in development was a practical response to the concurrent slowdown in orchestra and venue size.[[116]](#cite_note-Sachs445-116) Despite this trend in traditional instruments, the development of new musical instruments exploded in the twentieth century, and the variety of instruments developed overshadows any prior period.[[114]](#cite_note-Remnant183-114)

The proliferation of [electricity](/wiki/Electricity "Electricity") in the 20th century led to a new category of musical instruments: electronic instruments, or [electrophones](/wiki/Electrophone "Electrophone").[[117]](#cite_note-Sachs447-117) The vast majority produced in the first half of the 20th century were what Sachs called "electromechanical instruments"; they have mechanical parts that produce sound vibrations picked up and amplified by electrical components. Examples include [Hammond organs](/wiki/Hammond_organ "Hammond organ") and [electric guitars](/wiki/Electric_guitar "Electric guitar").[[117]](#cite_note-Sachs447-117) Sachs also defined a subcategory of "radioelectric instruments" such as the [theremin](/wiki/Theremin "Theremin"), which produces music through the player's hand movements around two [antennas](/wiki/Antenna_(radio) "Antenna (radio)").[[118]](#cite_note-Sachs448-118)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Moog_Modular_55_img2.jpg/250px-Moog_Modular_55_img2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Moog_Modular_55_img2.jpg)

A 1975 [Moog](/wiki/Moog_synthesizer "Moog synthesizer") Modular 55 synthesizer

The latter half of the 20th century saw the evolution of [synthesizers](/wiki/Synthesizer "Synthesizer"), which produce sound using circuits and [microchips](/wiki/Microchips "Microchips"). In the late 1960s, [Bob Moog](/wiki/Bob_Moog "Bob Moog") and other inventors developed the first commercial synthesizers, such as the [Moog synthesizer](/wiki/Moog_synthesizer "Moog synthesizer").[[119]](#cite_note-Pinch7-119) Whereas once they had filled rooms, synthesizers can now be embedded in any electronic device,[[119]](#cite_note-Pinch7-119) and are ubiquitous in modern music.[[120]](#cite_note-:03-120) [Samplers](/wiki/Sampler_(musical_instrument) "Sampler (musical instrument)"), introduced around 1980, allow users to [sample](/wiki/Sampling_(music) "Sampling (music)") and reuse existing sounds, and were important to the development of [hip hop](/wiki/Hip_hop "Hip hop").[[121]](#cite_note-121) 1982 saw the introduction of [MIDI](/wiki/MIDI "MIDI"), a [standardized](/wiki/Standardization "Standardization") means of synchronizing electronic instruments.[[122]](#cite_note-122) The modern proliferation of computers and microchips has created an industry of electronic musical instruments.[[123]](#cite_note-Manning168-123)

## Classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Musical_instrument&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Classification")]

Main article: [Musical instrument classification](/wiki/Musical_instrument_classification "Musical instrument classification")

There are many different methods of classifying musical instruments. Various methods examine aspects such as the physical properties of the instrument (material, color, shape, etc.), the use for the instrument, the means by which music is produced with the instrument, the [range](/wiki/Range_(music) "Range (music)") of the instrument, and the instrument's place in an [orchestra](/wiki/Orchestra "Orchestra") or other ensemble. Most methods are specific to a geographic area or cultural group and were developed to serve the unique classification requirements of the group.[[124]](#cite_note-Montagu210-124) The problem with these specialized classification schemes is that they tend to break down once they are applied outside of their original area. For example, a system based on instrument use would fail if a culture invented a new use for the same instrument. Scholars recognize [Hornbostel–Sachs](/wiki/Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs "Hornbostel–Sachs") as the only system that applies to any culture and, more importantly, provides the only possible classification for each instrument.[[125]](#cite_note-Montagu211-125)[[126]](#cite_note-Kartomi176-126) The most common classifications are [strings](/wiki/String_instrument "String instrument"), [brass](/wiki/Brass_instrument "Brass instrument"), [woodwind](/wiki/Woodwind_instrument "Woodwind instrument"), and [percussion](/wiki/Percussion_instrument "Percussion instrument").

### Ancient systems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Musical_instrument&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Ancient systems")]

An ancient Hindu system named the *[Natya Shastra](/wiki/Natya_Shastra "Natya Shastra")*, written by the sage [Bharata Muni](/wiki/Bharata_Muni "Bharata Muni") and dating from between 200 BC and 200 AD, divides instruments into four main classification groups: instruments where the sound is produced by vibrating strings; percussion instruments with skin heads; instruments where the sound is produced by vibrating columns of air; and "solid", or non-skin, percussion instruments.[[125]](#cite_note-Montagu211-125) This system was similar to some degree in 12th-century Europe by [Johannes de Muris](/wiki/Johannes_de_Muris "Johannes de Muris"), who used the terms *tensibilia* (stringed instruments), *inflatibilia* (wind instruments), and *percussibilia* (all percussion instruments).[[127]](#cite_note-Rault190-127) In 1880, [Victor-Charles Mahillon](/wiki/Victor-Charles_Mahillon "Victor-Charles Mahillon") adapted the *Natya Shastra* and assigned Greek labels to the four classifications: *chordophones* (stringed instruments), *membranophones* (skin-head percussion instruments), *aerophones* (wind instruments), and *autophones* (non-skin percussion instruments).[[125]](#cite_note-Montagu211-125)

### Hornbostel–Sachs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Musical_instrument&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Hornbostel–Sachs")]

[Erich von Hornbostel](/wiki/Erich_von_Hornbostel "Erich von Hornbostel") and [Curt Sachs](/wiki/Curt_Sachs "Curt Sachs") adopted Mahillon's scheme and published an extensive new scheme for classification in *Zeitschrift für Ethnologie* in 1914. Hornbostel and Sachs used most of Mahillon's system, but replaced the term *autophone* with *idiophone*.[[125]](#cite_note-Montagu211-125)

The original [Hornbostel–Sachs](/wiki/Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs "Hornbostel–Sachs") system classified instruments into four main groups:

* [Idiophones](/wiki/Idiophone "Idiophone"), which produce sound by vibrating the primary body of the instrument itself; they are sorted into concussion, percussion, shaken, scraped, split, and plucked idiophones, such as [claves](/wiki/Claves "Claves"), [xylophone](/wiki/Xylophone "Xylophone"), [guiro](/wiki/Guiro "Guiro"), [slit drum](/wiki/Slit_drum "Slit drum"), [mbira](/wiki/Mbira "Mbira"), and [rattle](/wiki/Rattle_(percussion_instrument) "Rattle (percussion instrument)").[[128]](#cite_note-Marcuse3-128)
* [Membranophones](/wiki/Membranophone "Membranophone"), which produce sound by a vibrating a stretched membrane; they may be [drums](/wiki/Drums "Drums") (further sorted by the shape of the shell), which are struck by hand, with a stick, or rubbed, but [kazoos](/wiki/Kazoos "Kazoos") and other instruments that use a stretched membrane for the primary sound (not simply to modify sound produced in another way) are also considered membranophones.[[129]](#cite_note-Marcuse117-129)
* [Chordophones](/wiki/Chordophone "Chordophone"), which produce sound by vibrating one or more strings; they are sorted according to the relationship between the string(s) and the sounding board or chamber. For example, if the strings are laid out parallel to the sounding board and there is no neck, the instrument is a [zither](/wiki/Zither "Zither") whether it is plucked like an [autoharp](/wiki/Autoharp "Autoharp") or struck with hammers like a [piano](/wiki/Piano "Piano"). If the instrument has strings parallel to the sounding board or chamber and the strings extend past the board with a neck, then the instrument is a [lute](/wiki/Lute "Lute"), whether the sound chamber is constructed of [wood](/wiki/Tonewood "Tonewood") like a [guitar](/wiki/Guitar "Guitar") or uses a membrane like a [banjo](/wiki/Banjo "Banjo").[[130]](#cite_note-Marcuse177-130)
* [Aerophones](/wiki/Aerophone "Aerophone"), which produce a sound with a vibrating column of air; they are sorted into free aerophones such as a [bullroarer](/wiki/Bullroarer "Bullroarer") or [whip](/wiki/Whip "Whip"), which move freely through the air; reedless aerophones such as [flutes](/wiki/Flute "Flute") and recorders, which cause the air to pass over a sharp edge; reed instruments, which use a vibrating reed (this category may be further divided into two classifications: single-reeded and double-reeded instruments. Examples of the former are clarinets and saxophones, while the latter includes oboes and bassoons); and lip-vibrated aerophones such as [trumpets](/wiki/Trumpet "Trumpet"), trombones and tubas, for which the lips themselves function as vibrating reeds.[[131]](#cite_note-Marcuse549-131)

Sachs later added a fifth category, [electrophones](/wiki/Electrophone "Electrophone"), such as [theremins](/wiki/Theremin "Theremin"), which produce sound by electronic means.[[117]](#cite_note-Sachs447-117) Within each category are many subgroups. The system has been criticised and revised over the years, but remains widely used by [ethnomusicologists](/wiki/Ethnomusicology "Ethnomusicology") and [organologists](/wiki/Organology "Organology").[[127]](#cite_note-Rault190-127)[[132]](#cite_note-Campbell39-132)

### Schaeffner

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Musical_instrument&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Schaeffner")]

Andre Schaeffner, a curator at the [Musée de l'Homme](/wiki/Mus%C3%A9e_de_l%27Homme "Musée de l'Homme"), disagreed with the Hornbostel–Sachs system and developed his own system in 1932. Schaeffner believed that the pure physics of a musical instrument, rather than its specific construction or playing method, should always determine its classification. (Hornbostel–Sachs, for example, divides aerophones on the basis of sound production, but membranophones on the basis of the shape of the instrument). His system divided instruments into two categories: instruments with solid, vibrating bodies and instruments containing vibrating air.[[133]](#cite_note-Kartomi174-133)

### Range

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Musical_instrument&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Range")]

Main article: [Range (music)](/wiki/Range_(music) "Range (music)")

Musical instruments are also often classified by their musical range in comparison with other instruments in the same family. This exercise is useful when placing instruments in context of an orchestra or other ensemble.

These terms are named after singing voice classifications:

* [Soprano](/wiki/Soprano "Soprano") instruments: [flute](/wiki/Flute "Flute"), [violin](/wiki/Violin "Violin"), [soprano saxophone](/wiki/Soprano_saxophone "Soprano saxophone"), [soprano sarrusophone](/wiki/Soprano_sarrusophone "Soprano sarrusophone"), [trumpet](/wiki/Trumpet "Trumpet"), [clarinet](/wiki/Clarinet "Clarinet"), [oboe](/wiki/Oboe "Oboe"), [piccolo](/wiki/Piccolo "Piccolo"), [glockenspiel](/wiki/Glockenspiel "Glockenspiel"), [celesta](/wiki/Celesta "Celesta")
* [Alto](/wiki/Alto "Alto") instruments: [alto saxophone](/wiki/Alto_saxophone "Alto saxophone"), [alto sarrusophone](/wiki/Alto_sarrusophone "Alto sarrusophone"), [french horn](/wiki/French_horn "French horn"), [alto flute](/wiki/Alto_flute "Alto flute"), [english horn](/wiki/English_horn "English horn"), [alto clarinet](/wiki/Alto_clarinet "Alto clarinet"), [viola](/wiki/Viola "Viola"), [alto horn](/wiki/Alto_horn "Alto horn"), [xylophone](/wiki/Xylophone "Xylophone"), [vibraphone](/wiki/Vibraphone "Vibraphone")
* [Tenor](/wiki/Tenor "Tenor") instruments: [trombone](/wiki/Trombone "Trombone"), [tenoroon](/wiki/Tenoroon "Tenoroon"), [tenor saxophone](/wiki/Tenor_saxophone "Tenor saxophone"), [tenor sarrusophone](/wiki/Tenor_sarrusophone "Tenor sarrusophone"), [tenor violin](/wiki/Tenor_violin "Tenor violin"), [guitar](/wiki/Guitar "Guitar"), [tenor drum](/wiki/Tenor_drum "Tenor drum"), [harpsichord](/wiki/Harpsichord "Harpsichord"), [harp](/wiki/Harp "Harp"), [marimba](/wiki/Marimba "Marimba")
* [Baritone](/wiki/Baritone "Baritone") instruments: [bassoon](/wiki/Bassoon "Bassoon"), [baritone saxophone](/wiki/Baritone_saxophone "Baritone saxophone"), [baritone sarrusophone](/wiki/Baritone_sarrusophone "Baritone sarrusophone"), [bass clarinet](/wiki/Bass_clarinet "Bass clarinet"), [cello](/wiki/Cello "Cello"), [baritone horn](/wiki/Baritone_horn "Baritone horn"), [euphonium](/wiki/Euphonium "Euphonium")
* [Bass](/wiki/Bass_(sound) "Bass (sound)") instruments: [double bass](/wiki/Double_bass "Double bass"), [bass guitar](/wiki/Bass_guitar "Bass guitar"), [contrabassoon](/wiki/Contrabassoon "Contrabassoon"), [bass saxophone](/wiki/Bass_saxophone "Bass saxophone"), [bass sarrusophone](/wiki/Bass_sarrusophone "Bass sarrusophone"), [tuba](/wiki/Tuba "Tuba"), [bass drum](/wiki/Bass_drum "Bass drum")

Some instruments fall into more than one category. For example, the cello may be considered tenor, baritone or bass, depending on how its music fits into the ensemble. The trombone and French horn may be alto, tenor, baritone, or bass depending on the range it is played in. Many instruments have their range as part of their name: [soprano saxophone](/wiki/Soprano_saxophone "Soprano saxophone"), [tenor saxophone](/wiki/Tenor_saxophone "Tenor saxophone"), [baritone horn](/wiki/Baritone_horn "Baritone horn"), [alto flute](/wiki/Alto_flute "Alto flute"), [bass guitar](/wiki/Bass_guitar "Bass guitar"), etc. Additional adjectives describe instruments above the soprano range or [below the bass](/wiki/Contrabass "Contrabass"), for example the [sopranino saxophone](/wiki/Sopranino_saxophone "Sopranino saxophone") and [contrabass clarinet](/wiki/Contrabass_clarinet "Contrabass clarinet"). When used in the name of an instrument, these terms are relative, describing the instrument's range in comparison to other instruments of its family and not in comparison to the human voice range or instruments of other families. For example, a bass flute's range is from C3 to F♯6, while a bass clarinet plays about one octave lower.

## Construction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Musical_instrument&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Construction")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/African_music_instrument_made_from_a_can_01.jpg/250px-African_music_instrument_made_from_a_can_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:African_music_instrument_made_from_a_can_01.jpg)

African [kalimba](/wiki/Mbira "Mbira") made from a food can

The materials used in making musical instruments vary greatly by culture and application. Many of the materials have special significance owing to their source or rarity. Some cultures worked substances from the human body into their instruments. In ancient Mexico, for example, the material drums were made from might contain actual human body parts obtained from sacrificial offerings. In New Guinea, drum makers would mix human blood into the adhesive used to attach the [membrane](/wiki/Acoustic_membrane "Acoustic membrane").[[134]](#cite_note-Rault184-134) [Mulberry](/wiki/Morus_(plant) "Morus (plant)") trees are held in high regard in China owing to their mythological significance—instrument makers would hence use them to make zithers. The [Yakuts](/wiki/Yakuts "Yakuts") believe that making drums from trees struck by lightning gives them a special connection to nature.[[135]](#cite_note-Rault185-135)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Five_string_kanteles.jpg/250px-Five_string_kanteles.jpg)](/wiki/File:Five_string_kanteles.jpg)

Two five string [Finnish](/wiki/Finland "Finland") [kanteles](/wiki/Kantele "Kantele"). Shape of the upper kantele is more traditional, while the one for kantele below is slightly modernised

Musical instrument construction is a specialized trade that requires years of training, practice, and sometimes an apprenticeship. Most makers of musical instruments specialize in one genre of instruments; for example, a [luthier](/wiki/Luthier "Luthier") makes only stringed instruments. Some make only one type of instrument such as a piano. Whatever the instrument constructed, the instrument maker must consider materials, construction technique, and decoration, creating a balanced instrument that is both functional and aesthetically pleasing.[[136]](#cite_note-Rault195-136) Some builders are focused on a more artistic approach and develop [experimental musical instruments](/wiki/Experimental_musical_instrument "Experimental musical instrument"), often meant for individual playing styles developed by the builder themself.

## User interfaces

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Musical_instrument&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: User interfaces")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Usnaconsole2.jpg/250px-Usnaconsole2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Usnaconsole2.jpg)

The five-manual, 522-stop detached console at the [United States Naval Academy Chapel](/wiki/Naval_Academy_Chapel "Naval Academy Chapel") crafted by R. A. Colby, Inc.[[137]](#cite_note-137)

Regardless of how the sound is produced, many musical instruments have a keyboard as the user interface. [Keyboard instruments](/wiki/Keyboard_instrument "Keyboard instrument") are any instruments that are played with a [musical keyboard](/wiki/Musical_keyboard "Musical keyboard"), which is a row of small keys that can be pressed. Every key generates one or more sounds; most keyboard instruments have extra means ([pedals](/wiki/Piano#Pedals "Piano") for a piano, [stops](/wiki/Organ_stop "Organ stop") and a [pedal keyboard](/wiki/Pedal_keyboard "Pedal keyboard") for an organ) to manipulate these sounds. They may produce sound by wind being fanned ([organ](/wiki/Organ_(music) "Organ (music)")) or pumped ([accordion](/wiki/Accordion "Accordion")),[[138]](#cite_note-bicknell-138)[[139]](#cite_note-howard-139) vibrating strings either hammered ([piano](/wiki/Piano "Piano")) or plucked ([harpsichord](/wiki/Harpsichord "Harpsichord")),[[140]](#cite_note-fine-140)[[141]](#cite_note-141) by electronic means ([synthesizer](/wiki/Synthesizer "Synthesizer")),[[142]](#cite_note-paradiso-142) or in some other way. Sometimes, instruments that do not usually have a keyboard, such as the *[glockenspiel](/wiki/Glockenspiel "Glockenspiel")*, are fitted with one.[[143]](#cite_note-143) Though they have no moving parts and are struck by mallets held in the player's hands, they have the same physical arrangement of keys and produce soundwaves in a similar manner. The [theremin](/wiki/Theremin "Theremin"), an [electrophone](/wiki/Electrophone "Electrophone"), is played without physical contact by the player. The theremin senses the proximity of the player's hands, which triggers changes in its sound. More recently, a [MIDI controller](/wiki/MIDI_controller "MIDI controller") keyboard used with a [digital audio workstation](/wiki/Digital_audio_workstation "Digital audio workstation") may have a musical keyboard and a bank of sliders, knobs, and buttons that change many sound parameters of a [synthesizer](/wiki/Synthesizer "Synthesizer").

Many musical instruments are able to be played with either right of left [handedness](/wiki/Handedness "Handedness"). However, some instruments can be made for the less frequent (≈10%) [left handedness](/wiki/Left_handedness "Left handedness"), such as [guitars](/wiki/Guitar "Guitar"). Well known left handed players include [Jimi Hendrix](/wiki/Jimi_Hendrix "Jimi Hendrix") and [Paul McCartney](/wiki/Paul_McCartney "Paul McCartney").

## Instrumentalist

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Musical_instrument&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Instrumentalist")]

"Instrumentalist" redirects here. For the philosophical school, see [Instrumentalism](/wiki/Instrumentalism "Instrumentalism"). For the magazine, see [The Instrumentalist](/wiki/The_Instrumentalist "The Instrumentalist").

See also: [Lists of musicians § Instrument](/wiki/Lists_of_musicians#Instrument "Lists of musicians"), and [Multi-instrumentalist](/wiki/Multi-instrumentalist "Multi-instrumentalist")

A person who plays a musical instrument is known as an instrumentalist or instrumental [musician](/wiki/Musician#Performer "Musician").[[144]](#cite_note-144)[[145]](#cite_note-145) Many instrumentalists are known for playing specific musical instruments such as [guitarist](/wiki/Guitarist "Guitarist") ([guitar](/wiki/Guitar "Guitar")), [pianist](/wiki/Pianist "Pianist") ([piano](/wiki/Piano "Piano")), [bassist](/wiki/Bassist "Bassist") ([bass](/wiki/Bass_(instrument) "Bass (instrument)")), and [drummer](/wiki/Drummer "Drummer") ([drum](/wiki/Drum "Drum")). These different types of instrumentalists can perform together in a music group.[[146]](#cite_note-146) A person who is able to play a number of instruments is called a multi-instrumentalist.[[147]](#cite_note-147) According to David Baskerville in the book *Music Business Handbook and Career Guide*, the working hours of a full-time instrumentalist may average only three hours a day, but most musicians spent at least forty hours a week.[[148]](#cite_note-148)
